DIVINE_COUNCIL: God "glorified" (ἐδόξασεν) His Servant — the same verb used for divine exaltation to heavenly glory.

The double negative construction (οὐκ... ἐν ἄλλῳ οὐδενί, "not in any other, no one") and the universal scope (ὑπὸ τὸν οὐρανόν, "under heaven," and ἐν ἀνθρώποις, "among men") exclude every alternative means of salvation. THEOLOGICAL: The prayer grounds its appeal in God's sovereignty as Creator — he who made heaven, earth, and sea rules over all earthly authorities. verses.flow: who through the Holy Spirit, by the mouth of our father David your servant, said: "Why did the nations rage, and the peoples imagine vain things?
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The community quotes Psalm 2:1-2 (LXX), one of the royal enthronement psalms. In its original context, it describes the nations conspiring against YHWH and his anointed king. The early church reads this christologically — the "Anointed One" (Χριστοῦ, Christou) is Jesus, and the psalm's fulfillment is narrated in vv. 27-28. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Psalm 2 is a foundational divine council text: YHWH installs his King on Zion despite the rebellion of earthly rulers. The apostolic prayer recognizes that the conspiracy of human and spiritual powers against the Lord's Anointed has been enacted in history through the crucifixion.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The prayer identifies the specific historical agents fulfilling Psalm 2: Herod (the "king"), Pontius Pilate (the "ruler"), the Gentiles (Roman soldiers and authorities), and the peoples of Israel. The phrase τὸν ἅγιον παῖδά σου Ἰησοῦν ("your holy Servant Jesus") uses παῖς (pais), which can mean both "servant" and "child," echoing the Servant Songs of Isaiah (Isa 42:1; 52:13). GREEK: ἔχρισας (echrisas) — "whom you anointed" — the verb χρίω (chriō, Strong's G5548) is the verbal root of Χριστός (Christos). Jesus is literally "the Anointed One" whom God himself anointed.

verses.flow: to do whatever your hand and your counsel predetermined to come to pass.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse expresses a profound theology of divine sovereignty over human evil. The verb προώρισεν (proōrisen, Strong's G4309) means "predetermined, decided beforehand" — the same word Paul uses in Romans 8:29-30 and Ephesians 1:5, 11. The crucifixion, though enacted by wicked hands (Acts 2:23), accomplished precisely what God's hand (χείρ) and counsel (βουλή) had foreordained. Human culpability and divine sovereignty are held in full tension without resolution.

The fresh filling with the Holy Spirit (ἐπλήσθησαν... τοῦ ἁγίου πνεύματος) is a second corporate filling after Pentecost, demonstrating that Spirit-filling is not a one-time event but a repeated empowerment for bold witness. verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Πειθαρχεῖν δεῖ θεῷ μᾶλλον ἢ ἀνθρώποις ("It is necessary to obey God rather than men") stands as a foundational principle of apostolic ethics. The verb πειθαρχεῖν (peitharchein, Strong's G3980) denotes obedience to a ruling authority — the apostles acknowledge legitimate human authority but declare God's authority supreme. This echoes Peter's earlier statement in Acts 4:19 and establishes the theological basis for civil disobedience when human commands contradict divine mandate.

verses.flow: The God of our fathers raised up Jesus, whom you killed, having hanged Him upon a tree.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The phrase κρεμάσαντες ἐπὶ ξύλου ("having hanged upon a tree/wood") deliberately invokes Deuteronomy 21:22-23 (LXX), where one hung on a tree bears God's curse. Paul develops this same typology in Galatians 3:13. Peter thus confronts the Sanhedrin with the paradox that the One they cursed by hanging on wood, God vindicated by raising from the dead. GREEK: ξύλον (xylon, Strong's G3586) means "wood, tree, stake" — the LXX word used in Deut 21:23 for the instrument of cursed execution.

verses.flow: This One, God exalted to His right hand as Author and Savior, to give repentance to Israel and forgiveness of sins.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: ἀρχηγός (archēgos, Strong's G747) means "author, originator, pioneer, prince" — a title laden with royal and soteriological weight, used also in Acts 3:15 and Hebrews 2:10; 12:2. Combined with σωτήρ (sōtēr, "Savior"), it ascribes to Jesus both the origin and the accomplishment of salvation. God's exalting Jesus to His right hand (τῇ δεξιᾷ αὐτοῦ) fulfills Psalm 110:1, the most-cited OT passage in the New Testament, establishing Christ's enthronement and messianic reign.

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: And we are witnesses of these words, and so is the Holy Spirit, whom God gave to those who obey Him."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Holy Spirit is presented as a co-witness alongside the apostles, affirming the Spirit's personhood and active role in testifying to Christ's resurrection and lordship. The condition for receiving the Spirit — obedience (τοῖς πειθαρχοῦσιν) — links back to the principle of v.29, creating a thematic unity: those who obey God receive the Spirit who testifies to the truth the apostles proclaim.

verses.flow: And those having heard were cut to the heart and were wanting to put them to death.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But a certain one in the council, a Pharisee named Gamaliel, a teacher of the law honored by all the people, stood up and commanded the men to be put outside for a little while.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Gamaliel (Γαμαλιήλ) was Gamaliel the Elder, grandson of the great Hillel, and one of the most revered Pharisaic teachers of the Second Temple period. He held the title Rabban ("our master"), a distinction above ordinary rabbi. The Mishnah (Sotah 9:15) says that when Gamaliel died, "the glory of the Torah ceased." Paul identifies himself as Gamaliel's student in Acts 22:3. His intervention here reflects the Pharisaic willingness, unlike the Sadducees, to allow for divine providence to vindicate or refute new movements.

verses.flow: And he said to them, "Men, Israelites, take heed to yourselves concerning these men, what you are about to do.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For before these days, Theudas rose up, claiming himself to be somebody, to whom a number of men, about four hundred, joined themselves; who was put to death, and all, as many as obeyed him, were scattered and came to nothing.
After this one, Judas the Galilean rose up in the days of the census and drew away people after him. That one also perished, and all, as many as obeyed him, were dispersed.
And now I say to you, withdraw from these men and let them alone; for if this counsel or this work is of men, it will be overthrown;
but if it is of God, you will not be able to overthrow them — lest you even be found to be fighters against God." And they were persuaded by him.
verses.note: GREEK: θεομάχοι (theomachoi, Strong's G2314) is a rare compound meaning "fighters against God" — a vivid term drawn from Greek literary tradition where mortals who oppose the gods meet catastrophic ends. This is its only NT occurrence. THEOLOGICAL: Gamaliel's counsel, while pragmatic rather than prophetic, ironically articulates a profound theological truth: what originates from God cannot be destroyed by human opposition, a principle that governs the entire narrative of Acts.

verses.flow: And having called the apostles to them, they beat them and commanded them not to speak upon the name of Jesus, and released them.

verses.flow: They therefore went out from the presence of the council, rejoicing that they were counted worthy to be dishonored on behalf of the Name.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The apostles' rejoicing at being dishonored "on behalf of the Name" (ὑπὲρ τοῦ ὀνόματος) reflects the paradoxical ethic Jesus taught in the Beatitudes (Matt 5:11-12). "The Name" (without further specification) became early Christian shorthand for the name of Jesus as Lord and Christ. GREEK: ἀτιμασθῆναι (atimasthēnai, from ἀτιμάζω, "to dishonor, treat shamefully") combined with κατηξιώθησαν ("counted worthy") creates a striking paradox — they were deemed worthy of unworthiness in the world's eyes.

verses.flow: And every day, in the temple and from house to house, they did not cease teaching and proclaiming the good news that Jesus is the Christ.
verses.note: GREEK: The phrase τὸν χριστὸν Ἰησοῦν ("the Christ, Jesus") with the article τόν makes χριστόν a title rather than merely a name — "that Jesus is the Anointed One." THEOLOGICAL: This verse summarizes the apostolic mission: ceaseless, public and domestic proclamation that Jesus is the promised Messiah. The combination of διδάσκοντες (teaching) and εὐαγγελιζόμενοι (proclaiming good news) shows the dual nature of early Christian witness — doctrinal instruction and kerygmatic proclamation, centered on the identity of Jesus as the Christ.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: Now in these days, as the disciples were multiplying, there arose a grumbling of the Hellenists against the Hebrews, because their widows were being overlooked in the daily ministry.
verses.note: GREEK: "Grumbling" (γογγυσμός, gongysmós, Strong's G1112) echoes the LXX language of Israel's murmuring in the wilderness (Exod 16:7-8; Num 11:1). "Hellenists" (Ἑλληνισταί) refers to Greek-speaking Jews of the Diaspora, distinct from Aramaic-speaking "Hebrews" (Ἑβραῖοι). HISTORICAL: This tension reflects a real sociolinguistic divide within early Jerusalem Christianity, where Diaspora Jews worshipped in Greek-language synagogues and may have lacked the local family networks that sustained Hebrew-speaking widows.

verses.flow: And the Twelve, having summoned the multitude of the disciples, said, "It is not pleasing that we, having forsaken the word of God, should serve tables.

verses.note: GREEK: "Well-attested" (μαρτυρουμένους, martyroumenous) is a passive participle indicating men whose character is publicly witnessed to by others. "Full of the Spirit and of wisdom" echoes the qualifications of Bezalel for tabernacle service (Exod 31:3). THEOLOGICAL: The apostles establish a principle that Spirit-filling is required not only for preaching but for practical service within the community of God.

verses.flow: But we ourselves will devote ourselves steadfastly to prayer and to the ministry of the word."

And the word was pleasing before all the multitude, and they chose Stephen, a man full of faith and of the Holy Spirit, and Philip, and Prochorus, and Nicanor, and Timon, and Parmenas, and Nicolaus, a proselyte of Antioch,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: All seven names are Greek, suggesting the apostles addressed the Hellenist complaint by selecting men from that very community. Nicolaus is specifically identified as a "proselyte" (προσήλυτος), a Gentile convert to Judaism who then became a follower of Christ — showing the expanding reach of the gospel even at this early stage. THEOLOGICAL: Stephen is distinguished as "full of faith and of the Holy Spirit," foreshadowing his central role in chapters 6-7.

verses.flow: whom they set before the apostles, and having prayed, they laid their hands upon them.
And the word of God was growing, and the number of the disciples in Jerusalem was multiplying greatly, and a great multitude of the priests were becoming obedient to the faith.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "the word of God was growing" (ὁ λόγος τοῦ θεοῦ ηὔξανεν) personifies the word almost as a living force, a pattern Luke repeats as a summary marker throughout Acts (Acts 12:24; 19:20). HISTORICAL: That "a great multitude of the priests were becoming obedient to the faith" is remarkable — these are serving Levitical priests in the Jerusalem temple, suggesting penetration of the gospel into the heart of Second Temple institutional Judaism.

verses.flow: And Stephen, full of grace and power, was performing wonders and great signs among the people.

verses.flow: But there arose certain ones from the synagogue called that of the Freedmen, and of the Cyrenians, and of the Alexandrians, and of those from Cilicia and Asia, disputing with Stephen.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Freedmen" (Λιβερτίνων, Libertīnōn, Strong's G3032) likely refers to descendants of Jews enslaved by Pompey in 63 BC and later freed in Rome, who returned to Jerusalem and established their own synagogue. Archaeological evidence (the Theodotus inscription) confirms such Diaspora synagogues existed in Jerusalem. The opponents from Cilicia may have included Saul of Tarsus (Acts 21:39; 22:3).

verses.flow: And they were not able to withstand the wisdom and the Spirit by which he was speaking.

verses.flow: Then they secretly instigated men saying, "We have heard him speaking blasphemous words against Moses and God."
verses.note: GREEK: "Secretly instigated" (ὑπέβαλον, hypébalon, Strong's G5260) is a hapax legomenon in the NT, meaning to suborn or put forward covertly — a term drawn from legal contexts implying the coaching of witnesses. TYPOLOGY: The bringing of false charges of blasphemy against Stephen closely parallels the trial of Jesus, where false witnesses likewise testified before the Sanhedrin (Matt 26:59-61; Mark 14:56-58).

verses.flow: And they stirred up the people and the elders and the scribes, and having come upon him, they seized him and brought him to the council,

verses.flow: For we have heard him saying that this Jesus the Nazarene will destroy this place and will change the customs which Moses delivered to us."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The charge that Jesus "will destroy this place" echoes the false testimony at Jesus' own trial (Mark 14:58; John 2:19-21). Ironically, Jesus did prophesy the temple's destruction (Matt 24:2), and his death and resurrection did inaugurate a new covenant that transformed the "customs which Moses delivered." THEOLOGICAL: The accusation, though distorted, touches on the genuine theological reality that the coming of Christ fulfills and transforms the Mosaic covenant and its temple cultus (Heb 8:13).

verses.flow: And gazing intently at him, all those sitting in the council saw his face as the face of an angel.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Stephen's face appearing "as the face of an angel" (ὡσεὶ πρόσωπον ἀγγέλου) recalls Moses' shining face after encountering God on Sinai (Exod 34:29-35). This is deeply ironic: Stephen is accused of blaspheming Moses, yet he bears the very glory-marks of Moses' own divine encounter. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The angelic radiance signals Stephen's participation in the heavenly council's glory, anticipating his vision of the Son of Man standing at God's right hand (Acts 7:55-56).

verses.flow: And the high priest said, "Are these things so?"

verses.flow: And he said, "Men, brothers and fathers, hear! The God of glory appeared to our father Abraham while he was in Mesopotamia, before he dwelt in Haran,
verses.note: GREEK: "Ὁ θεὸς τῆς δόξης" (ho theos tēs doxēs) — "The God of glory" is a title drawn from Psalm 29:3 (LXX 28:3). Stephen begins his defense by invoking God's transcendent glory, emphasizing that God's presence is not confined to a single land or temple. THEOLOGICAL: Stephen's entire speech argues that God revealed Himself outside the land of Israel — first in Mesopotamia — undermining the Sanhedrin's temple-centric theology.

verses.flow: and said to him, "Go out from your land and from your kindred, and come into the land which I shall show you."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The call of Abraham from Ur (Gen 12:1; cf. Gen 15:7; Neh 9:7) establishes the pattern of God calling His people out of pagan lands into covenant relationship. This anticipates the greater exodus in Christ. GREEK: "δεῦρο" (deuro) is an adverbial imperative meaning "come here/hither," preserving the urgent personal summons of God.

verses.flow: Then having gone out from the land of the Chaldeans, he dwelt in Haran. And from there, after his father died, God removed him into this land in which you now dwell.

verses.flow: And He gave him no inheritance in it, not even a footstep, yet He promised to give it to him for a possession, and to his seed after him, though he had no child.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The paradox of promise without possession is central to Stephen's argument — God's faithfulness operates through promise and patience, not immediate territorial fulfillment. Abraham held the promise by faith alone (cf. Heb 11:8-10). GREEK: "βῆμα ποδός" (bēma podos) — literally "a step of a foot," an idiom for the smallest measure of land (cf. Deut 2:5 LXX).

verses.flow: And God spoke thus: that his seed would be sojourners in a foreign land, and they would enslave them and afflict them four hundred years.
"And the nation to which they shall be in bondage I will judge," said God, "and after these things they shall come out and shall serve Me in this place."
And He gave him a covenant of circumcision; and so he begot Isaac and circumcised him on the eighth day; and Isaac begot Jacob, and Jacob the twelve patriarchs.
verses.note: GREEK: "διαθήκην περιτομῆς" (diathēkēn peritomēs) — "covenant of circumcision," a genitive of characterization; circumcision is the sign sealing the covenant (Gen 17:10-14). THEOLOGICAL: Stephen traces the covenant line through the patriarchs, showing that God's covenant preceded both the law and the temple.

In which time Moses was born, and he was beautiful before God; and he was nourished three months in the house of his father.
verses.note: GREEK: "ἀστεῖος τῷ θεῷ" (asteios tō theō) — "beautiful before God" or "fair to God"; the dative τῷ θεῷ functions as a Hebraic superlative, meaning "exceedingly beautiful" or "divinely beautiful" (cf. Exod 2:2 LXX; Heb 11:23). HISTORICAL: Moses' birth during the period of infanticide parallels the birth of Christ during Herod's massacre (Matt 2:16).

verses.flow: And having seen a certain one being wronged, he defended him and executed vengeance for the one being oppressed, having struck down the Egyptian.
And he supposed that his brothers would understand that God through his hand was giving deliverance to them; but they did not understand.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Moses as rejected deliverer is a type of Christ — both were sent by God to bring salvation, both were rejected by their own people. Stephen draws this parallel explicitly to indict the Sanhedrin. THEOLOGICAL: "σωτηρίαν" (sōtērian) — "deliverance/salvation" — God was giving rescue through Moses' hand, yet Israel failed to perceive it, just as they now fail to perceive God's salvation through Christ.

This angel is identified in v.31-32 as speaking with the voice of the Lord and declaring "I am the God of your fathers." This reflects the Angel of YHWH theology of the Old Testament, where a divine messenger speaks as God Himself (cf. Exod 3:2-6). THEOLOGICAL: The theophany at Sinai occurs in the wilderness, far from any temple — reinforcing Stephen's argument that God's presence transcends sacred architecture.

And the Lord said to him, "Loose the sandal from your feet, for the place on which you stand is holy ground.

verses.flow: "Seeing I have seen the affliction of My people who are in Egypt, and I have heard their groaning, and I have come down to deliver them. And now, come, I will send you into Egypt."

verses.flow: This Moses whom they refused, saying, "Who appointed you a ruler and a judge?"—this one God sent as both ruler and redeemer, with the hand of the angel who appeared to him in the bush.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The double rejection pattern — "this Moses whom they refused... this one God sent as ruler and redeemer" — is the definitive type for Christ's rejection and exaltation. The Greek "λυτρωτήν" (lytrōtēn, Strong's G3086) means "redeemer/ransomer," used only here in the NT, echoing the exodus redemption now fulfilled in Christ. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Moses is sent "with the hand of the angel" (σὺν χειρὶ ἀγγέλου) — the angel of the bush, linking the divine council agent to the work of deliverance.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: This one led them out, having done wonders and signs in the land of Egypt, and in the Red Sea, and in the wilderness for forty years.

verses.flow: This is the Moses who said to the sons of Israel, "A Prophet God shall raise up for you from your brothers, as He raised me."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Stephen quotes Deut 18:15 (LXX), the prophecy of the Prophet like Moses. This is a direct messianic claim: Jesus is this Prophet whom God raised up. The phrase "ὡς ἐμέ" (hōs eme, "as me") establishes a typological correspondence between Moses and the coming Messiah. TYPOLOGY: Moses as mediator, lawgiver, and deliverer foreshadows Christ who is the greater mediator (Heb 8:6), the giver of the new covenant, and the ultimate Redeemer.

verses.flow: This is the one who was in the assembly in the wilderness with the angel who spoke to him on Mount Sinai, and with our fathers; who received living oracles to give to us,
verses.note: GREEK: "λόγια ζῶντα" (logia zōnta) — "living oracles." "Lógia" (Strong's G3051) denotes divine utterances or sayings. The modifier "living" emphasizes the enduring, active power of God's word (cf. Heb 4:12; 1 Pet 1:23). DIVINE_COUNCIL: Moses stood in the assembly (ἐκκλησία) with "the angel who spoke to him" — the divine mediator at Sinai, connecting angelic mediation of the law with the divine council tradition (cf. Gal 3:19; Heb 2:2).

verses.flow: to whom our fathers were not willing to become obedient, but thrust him away and turned back in their hearts to Egypt,

And they made a calf in those days, and brought a sacrifice to the idol, and were rejoicing in the works of their hands.

And God turned and gave them over to serve the host of heaven, as it has been written in the book of the prophets: "Did you offer to Me slain beasts and sacrifices for forty years in the wilderness, O house of Israel?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: God "turned and gave them over" (ἔστρεψεν... παρέδωκεν) — divine judicial handing-over, the same pattern Paul describes in Rom 1:24-28. Idolatry leads to God releasing people to the consequences of their rebellion. DIVINE_COUNCIL: "τῇ στρατιᾷ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ" (tē stratia tou ouranou) — "the host of heaven," referring to celestial beings worshipped as gods by the nations (Deut 4:19; 32:8-9 LXX). Stephen connects Israel's apostasy to the worship of divine council members allotted to the nations.

verses.flow: And you took up the tent of Moloch and the star of your god Rephan, the images which you made to worship them. And I will remove you beyond Babylon."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Stephen quotes Amos 5:25-27 (LXX), substituting "Babylon" for the MT's "Damascus," reflecting either a LXX variant or a prophetic reapplication to the Babylonian exile. "Moloch" and "Rephan" (Ῥαιφάν, a LXX rendering of the Assyrian astral deity) represent Israel's persistent idolatry. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "star of your god Rephan" connects to astral worship of divine council members — beings God assigned over the nations (Deut 32:8 LXX) but which Israel was forbidden to worship.

verses.flow: The tabernacle of the testimony was with our fathers in the wilderness, as He who spoke to Moses directed him to make it according to the pattern which he had seen.

verses.flow: Which also our fathers, having received in succession, brought in with Joshua into the possession of the nations, whom God drove out from the face of our fathers, until the days of David,
who found favor before God and asked to find a dwelling place for the God of Jacob.
But Solomon built Him a house.
Yet the Most High does not dwell in things made by hands, as the prophet says:
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "ὁ ὕψιστος" (ho hypsistos) — "the Most High," a divine title emphasizing God's supreme sovereignty over all heavenly and earthly powers (cf. Gen 14:18-22; Ps 82:6; Dan 7:18). Stephen's point is devastating: the Most High cannot be contained in structures made by human hands. This does not reject the temple but relativizes it. GREEK: "χειροποιήτοις" (cheiropoiētois) — "things made by hands," a term often used in the LXX for idols (Isa 2:18; 19:1), carrying a sharp polemical edge.

verses.flow: "Heaven is My throne, and the earth is a footstool for My feet. What kind of house will you build for Me?" says the Lord. "Or what is the place of My rest?
Did not My hand make all these things?"
Stiff-necked and uncircumcised in hearts and ears! You always resist the Holy Spirit; as your fathers did, so also do you.
verses.note: GREEK: "Σκληροτράχηλοι" (sklērotráchēloi, Strong's G4644) — "stiff-necked," a hapax legomenon in the NT, drawn from the LXX of Exod 33:3, 5; 34:9. "ἀπερίτμητοι καρδίαις καὶ τοῖς ὠσίν" — "uncircumcised in hearts and ears" echoes Jer 6:10; 9:26; Ezek 44:7. THEOLOGICAL: Stephen turns from historical recitation to direct prophetic indictment. The charge of resisting the Holy Spirit frames Israel's entire history as a pattern of opposition to God's Spirit working through His appointed agents.

words.m: verb aorist active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: Which of the prophets did your fathers not persecute? And they killed those who announced beforehand concerning the coming of the Righteous One, of whom you have now become betrayers and murderers—
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "τοῦ δικαίου" (tou dikaiou) — "the Righteous One," a messianic title (cf. Isa 53:11; Acts 3:14; 22:14; 1 John 2:1). Stephen charges the Sanhedrin with being the culmination of Israel's prophets-killing tradition, now climaxing in the betrayal and murder of the Messiah Himself. TYPOLOGY: The pattern of prophet-rejection throughout Israel's history reaches its apex in the rejection of Christ — the ultimate Prophet, the Righteous One foretold.

verses.flow: you who received the law as ordinances of angels, and did not keep it.

Notably, Jesus is "standing" (ἑστῶτα) rather than "sitting" — possibly indicating Christ rising to receive His martyr, or standing as advocate/witness. verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Peter's rhetorical question — "Can anyone forbid the water?" — argues from the greater to the lesser: if God has already given the greater gift (the Holy Spirit), who can withhold the lesser sign (water baptism)? If Hellēnistas is original, these are Greek-speaking Jews; if Hellēnas, these are pagan Gentiles. The context of the chapter strongly favors the reading that refers to Gentiles, marking the first intentional Gentile evangelism outside of direct divine intervention.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The angel of the Lord who delivered Peter (v. 7) now strikes Herod — demonstrating that the same divine agent who saves the faithful judges the blasphemous. HISTORICAL: Josephus (Antiquities 19.8.2) corroborates this account, recording that Agrippa appeared in silver garments at Caesarea, was hailed as a god, and died five days later of severe abdominal pain. verses.note: STRUCTURE: This summary statement forms a literary hinge in Acts. Despite — and precisely through — Herod's persecution, the word of God (ho logos tou theou) grows and multiplies. Luke employs the same growth language used for Israel's multiplication in Egypt (Exod 1:7 LXX), reinforcing the new-exodus typology pervading this chapter. The contrast is stark: Herod dies, but the word lives.

verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul's sermon recapitulates Israel's salvation history, following the pattern of Stephen's speech (Acts 7). The "uplifted arm" (μετὰ βραχίονος ὑψηλοῦ) is a direct LXX allusion to the Exodus tradition (Deut 26:8; Ex 6:6), presenting God as the divine warrior who acts with sovereign power on behalf of His chosen people. verses.flow: And that He raised Him from the dead, no longer being about to return to corruption, thus He has spoken: "I will give to you the holy and faithful things of David."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "The holy and faithful things of David" (τὰ ὅσια Δαυὶδ τὰ πιστά) quotes Isaiah 55:3 LXX, referring to the everlasting covenant promises made to David. Paul's argument is that these promises find their fulfillment not in David himself (who died and decayed, v36) but in the risen Christ, whose body did not see corruption. The incorruptible resurrection body is the guarantee that the Davidic covenant stands forever.

verses.flow: Therefore also in another He says, "You will not give Your Holy One to see corruption."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This quotation of Psalm 16:10 (LXX 15:10) is the same text Peter applied to Jesus in Acts 2:27. GREEK: "Holy One" (ὅσιον, hosion, G3741) denotes one who is divinely consecrated and faithful — distinct from hagios in its connotation of covenant loyalty. Paul and Peter both argue that David spoke prophetically of Christ, since David himself underwent bodily decay.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is the doctrinal climax of the sermon. "Forgiveness of sins" (ἄφεσις ἁμαρτιῶν) is proclaimed through Christ alone. GREEK: "Proclaimed" (καταγγέλλεται, katangelletai, G2605) is present passive — the forgiveness is being continuously heralded as an ongoing reality through the preaching of the gospel. The linkage to v39 establishes justification by faith as surpassing what the Mosaic law could accomplish.

The law of Moses could not justify (δικαιωθῆναι, dikaiōthēnai, G1344 — to be declared righteous), but in Christ "everyone who believes is justified." This anticipates the fuller exposition in Romans 3:20-28 and Galatians 2:16. The scope is universal — πᾶς ὁ πιστεύων, "everyone who believes" — with no ethnic or legal restriction.

Those who refuse to believe will experience the same astonishment and destruction as faithless Judah. verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This pivotal declaration establishes the theological pattern of salvation history: "to you first" (ὑμῖν... πρῶτον) — the word must go first to Israel, then to the nations (cf. Rom 1:16). Israel's rejection does not thwart God's plan but rather occasions its expansion to the Gentiles. STRUCTURE: This verse marks the major turning point of Acts 13 and anticipates the broader Gentile mission that dominates the rest of Acts.

verses.flow: For thus the Lord has commanded us: "I have set you as a light of the Gentiles, that you should be for salvation unto the ends of the earth."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The quotation from Isaiah 49:6 originally addressed the Servant of the Lord. Paul applies it to the apostolic mission, indicating that the church participates in the Servant's vocation as a "light of the Gentiles" (φῶς ἐθνῶν). DIVINE_COUNCIL: The Servant of Isaiah 49 reverses the disinheritance of the nations described in Deuteronomy 32:8-9 — the God of Israel now reclaims the nations through His Anointed One and His commissioned agents. "Unto the ends of the earth" echoes Acts 1:8.

verses.flow: And hearing this, the Gentiles were rejoicing and were glorifying the word of the Lord, and as many as had been appointed to eternal life believed.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "As many as had been appointed to eternal life believed" (ὅσοι ἦσαν τεταγμένοι εἰς ζωὴν αἰώνιον). GREEK: "Appointed" (τεταγμένοι, tetagmenoi, G5021) is a perfect passive participle of tassō, meaning "to arrange, assign, appoint." The passive voice indicates divine action — these had been previously arranged by God for eternal life. This is one of the clearest statements of divine election in Acts, balancing the free offer of v38-39 with the sovereignty of God in salvation.

verses.flow: And the crowds, having seen what Paul did, lifted up their voices in the Lycaonian language, saying, "The gods, having been made like men, have come down to us!"
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Lycaonia was a region in south-central Asia Minor where the local language persisted alongside Greek. The Lycaonian language (Λυκαονιστί, a hapax legomenon in the NT, G3072) explains why Paul and Barnabas did not immediately understand the crowd's intent. The identification with gods descending in human form reflects a local tradition preserved in Ovid's account of Zeus and Hermes visiting the nearby region of Phrygia (Metamorphoses 8.611-724).

verses.flow: And they were calling Barnabas Zeus, and Paul Hermes, since he was the chief speaker.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The Lycaonians' identification of Barnabas as Zeus and Paul as Hermes reveals the pagan divine council framework — a pantheon of gods who descend to interact with mortals. Paul's response in vv. 15-17 directly confronts this theology by redirecting worship to the one living God (θεὸν ζῶντα), the Creator, echoing the Deuteronomy 32:8-9 framework where the Most High allotted nations to lesser beings while claiming Israel as His own.

He appeals to natural revelation — God as Creator of heaven, earth, and sea (echoing Exod 20:11; Ps 146:6). The phrase θεὸν ζῶντα ("the living God") stands in deliberate contrast to the "vain things" (τῶν ματαίων) of idol worship, a term used in the LXX for empty idols (Jer 2:5; 1 Sam 12:21).

This is the earliest account of structured church governance with πρεσβυτέρους (elders) being formally appointed in Gentile congregations. STRUCTURE: The pattern of prayer, fasting, and commending to the Lord mirrors the commissioning at Antioch (Acts 13:2-3), creating a literary bookend to the first missionary journey.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase θύραν πίστεως ("a door of faith") is theologically significant — God Himself is the agent who opens (ἤνοιξεν) the door to the Gentiles. This echoes the Cornelius episode (Acts 10-11) and anticipates the Jerusalem Council debate (Acts 15). Faith, not Torah observance, is the entrance point for Gentile inclusion in God's people (cf. 1 Cor 16:9; 2 Cor 2:12; Col 4:3).

verses.flow: And certain men, having come down from Judea, were teaching the brothers that, "Unless you are circumcised according to the custom of Moses, you are not able to be saved."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse introduces the central crisis of the early assembly: whether Gentile believers must become proselytes to Judaism (through circumcision per the Mosaic covenant) in order to be saved. This is not merely a ritual question but a soteriological one—whether salvation comes through Torah-observance or through the grace of Christ alone. HISTORICAL: These teachers likely came from the strict Pharisaic wing of the Jerusalem assembly, reflecting tensions within Second Temple Judaism over the status of God-fearing Gentiles.

verses.flow: But certain of those from the sect of the Pharisees who had believed rose up, saying, "It is necessary to circumcise them and to command them to keep the law of Moses."
verses.note: GREEK: The word haíresis (G139) here means "sect" or "party," not "heresy" in the later ecclesiastical sense. These were believing Pharisees—genuine followers of Christ who nonetheless maintained that Torah-observance, including circumcision, was binding on all believers. HISTORICAL: The Pharisaic movement was known for its meticulous adherence to oral and written Torah; their insistence here reflects a natural extension of their pre-conversion convictions into their new faith.

verses.flow: And after much debate had taken place, Peter, having risen up, said to them, "Men, brothers, you yourselves know that from early days God made choice among you, that through my mouth the Gentiles should hear the word of the gospel and believe.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Peter appeals to the divine initiative in the Cornelius episode (Acts 10-11) as authoritative precedent. God Himself chose (exeléxato, G1586, aorist middle) to send the gospel to the Gentiles through Peter's mouth. The phrase "from early days" (aph' hēmerōn archaíōn) indicates that this was not a recent innovation but part of God's established plan.

verses.flow: And God, the Knower of hearts, bore witness to them, having given them the Holy Spirit, just as also to us,
verses.note: GREEK: Kardiognōstēs (G2589) is a rare compound word meaning "Knower of hearts," occurring only here and in Acts 1:24 in the entire NT. It is a hapax legomenon in broader Greek literature as well. THEOLOGICAL: God's testimony is His sovereign act of giving the Holy Spirit to uncircumcised Gentiles, thereby validating their faith apart from Torah-works. The Spirit's indwelling is the divine seal of acceptance.

verses.flow: and He made no distinction between us and them, having cleansed their hearts by faith.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The cleansing of hearts "by faith" (tē pístei, dative of means) is Peter's critical assertion: ritual purity through circumcision and Torah is superseded by the inward cleansing that comes through faith. God made "no distinction" (outhén diékrinen)—the same verb used in Acts 11:12 of Peter's vision. This levels the covenantal playing field between Jew and Gentile.

verses.flow: Now therefore, why do you test God, to place a yoke upon the neck of the disciples which neither our fathers nor we were able to bear?
But through the grace of the Lord Jesus we believe to be saved, in the same manner as they also."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is remarkably bold in its formulation. Peter does not say Gentiles are saved as Jews are, but rather that "we" (Jews) are saved in the same manner as "they" (Gentiles)—that is, through grace alone. This inverts the expected hierarchy and places all humanity on equal footing before the grace of the Lord Jesus. TYPOLOGY: The "yoke" (zygós, v.10) imagery echoes Jesus' teaching in Matt 11:29-30, where He offers His own light yoke in contrast to the heavy burden of Pharisaic interpretation.

verses.flow: "After these things I will return, and I will rebuild the tent of David which has fallen, and its ruins I will rebuild, and I will raise it up again,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: James quotes Amos 9:11-12 (LXX), identifying the restoration of the "tent of David" (tēn skēnēn Dauíd) with what God is now doing through Christ. The fallen Davidic dynasty is being rebuilt not through political restoration but through the Messiah's kingship extending to all nations. The LXX reading differs significantly from the MT here, using "seek the Lord" where the Hebrew reads "possess the remnant of Edom." DIVINE_COUNCIL: The rebuilt tent/tabernacle of David signals the Messianic kingdom inaugurated by Jesus, fulfilling the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7:12-16) and extending divine rule over the nations (Ps 2:7-8).

This grounds the Gentile mission in the eternal purpose of God.

THEOLOGICAL: Physical liberation serves as a sign of the spiritual liberation proclaimed in the gospel message that follows (vv.30-31).

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Πίστευσον ἐπὶ τὸν κύριον Ἰησοῦν ("Believe upon the Lord Jesus") is the most concise gospel proclamation in Acts. verses.flow: But Paul said to them, "Having beaten us publicly, uncondemned, men who are Romans, they cast us into prison; and now they are casting us out secretly? No indeed! But let them come themselves and lead us out."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Roman citizenship (civitas Romana) carried extraordinary legal protections. The lex Valeria (509 BC) and lex Porcia (c. 195 BC) prohibited binding and beating Roman citizens without trial. Violation was a serious offense that could end a magistrate's career. Paul's invocation of this right is not mere self-interest but ensures the protection and dignity of the fledgling Christian community in Philippi. GREEK: ἀκατακρίτους (akatakritous, "uncondemned," Strong's G178) appears only here and in Acts 22:25 in the NT.

This twofold proclamation to both Jews and Greeks demonstrates the universal scope of the gospel. The pairing of repentance and faith as inseparable aspects of conversion is foundational to apostolic preaching (Mark 1:15).

verses.note: GREEK: δεδεμένος τῷ πνεύματι ("bound in the Spirit") — the perfect passive participle δεδεμένος indicates a settled state of constraint. Whether this refers to Paul's own spirit or the Holy Spirit is debated, but the parallel with v.23 where the Holy Spirit explicitly testifies suggests divine compulsion. THEOLOGICAL: Paul models the life of one under the sovereign direction of the Spirit, pressing forward despite certain suffering — a pattern echoing Christ's own determined journey toward Jerusalem (Luke 9:51).

verses.flow: And now, behold, I know that you all, among whom I went about proclaiming the kingdom, shall see my face no more.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: This verse marks the emotional turning point of the Miletus address. Paul's declaration that they will "see my face no more" gives the speech the character of a farewell testament, a well-known genre in Jewish literature (cf. Jacob in Gen 49, Moses in Deut 31-33, Joshua in Josh 23-24). The mention of "proclaiming the kingdom" (κηρύσσων τὴν βασιλείαν) connects Paul's ministry to the central message of Jesus Himself (Luke 4:43).

verses.flow: Therefore I testify to you on this day that I am clean from the blood of all,

verses.note: GREEK: βουλήν τοῦ θεοῦ ("counsel of God") — βουλή (boulē, Strong's G1012) denotes the deliberate, sovereign plan and purpose of God, not merely advice. This is the same term Luke uses for God's predetermined plan in Acts 2:23 and 4:28. THEOLOGICAL: Paul claims to have proclaimed the totality of the divine counsel without holding anything back (οὐ ὑπεστειλάμην, "I did not shrink back"), establishing the standard for faithful pastoral ministry.

verses.flow: Take heed to yourselves and to all the flock, in which the Holy Spirit has appointed you overseers, to shepherd the assembly of God, which He purchased through His own blood.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is one of the most doctrinally dense verses in Acts. The Holy Spirit Himself appoints overseers (ἐπισκόπους, episkopous) — establishing that church leadership is a divine, not merely human, appointment. The term ἐπίσκοπος is used interchangeably with πρεσβύτερος (v.17), indicating that "elder" and "overseer" denote the same office. GREEK: The phrase "through His own blood" (διὰ τοῦ αἵματος τοῦ ἰδίου) applied to "the assembly of God" (τὴν ἐκκλησίαν τοῦ θεοῦ) is a profound assertion: God purchased the church through His own blood — a powerful affirmation of the deity of Christ, whose blood is identified as God's own. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The imagery of God as shepherd over His flock echoes Ezek 34, where God Himself would shepherd Israel, displacing unfaithful shepherds — here fulfilled through Christ and delegated to Spirit-appointed overseers.

These align with the Noahide laws and the requirements for resident aliens (גֵּרִים) in Leviticus 17-18.

verses.flow: But falling upon a place where two seas met, they ran the vessel aground; and the bow, having stuck fast, remained immovable, but the stern was being broken apart by the violence of the waves.
verses.note: GREEK: The term διθάλασσον (dithalasson, G1337) means literally "between two seas" — a place where converging currents or a sandbar creates a shoal. This is a NT hapax legomenon. The verb ἐρείσασα (ereisasa, "having stuck fast") is also rare, conveying the bow being driven immovably into the seabed.

verses.flow: But the centurion, wishing to bring Paul safely through, kept them from their purpose, and commanded those able to swim, having cast themselves off first, to go out upon the land,
and the rest, some upon planks, and some upon pieces from the ship. And so it came about that all were brought safely to the land.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Luke's concluding statement that πάντας διασωθῆναι ("all were brought safely through") fulfills the angel's promise in v.24 with precise completeness. The verb διασῴζω (G1295) carries the force of bringing safely through peril. TYPOLOGY: The deliverance of all through water on account of one righteous man echoes the Noah narrative (Gen 7:1; 1 Pet 3:20) and prefigures the comprehensive salvation available through Christ, the one through whom God purposes to save many (Rom 5:15-19).

verses.flow: And the native people showed us no ordinary kindness, for having kindled a fire, they received us all because of the rain that had set in and because of the cold.
verses.note: GREEK: βάρβαροι (barbaroi, G915) does not mean "barbarians" in the modern pejorative sense but refers to non-Greek-speaking peoples. The term φιλανθρωπίαν (philanthrōpian, G5363) — literally "love of humanity" — is a notable word indicating genuine compassion. HISTORICAL: Malta's inhabitants at this time spoke a Punic dialect, thus qualifying as barbaroi to Greek speakers.

verses.flow: And when Paul had gathered a bundle of sticks and laid them upon the fire, a viper came out from the heat and fastened upon his hand.

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative singular neuter
verb aorist passive participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: And when the native people saw the creature hanging from his hand, they said to one another, "Surely this man is a murderer, whom, though he was brought safely from the sea, Justice has not permitted to live."
verses.note: GREEK: ἡ δίκη (hē dikē, G1349) — "Justice" — is here personified as a divine figure of retribution. HISTORICAL: This reflects Greco-Roman popular theology in which Dikē was a goddess who pursued murderers. The native Maltese shared this common Mediterranean belief in divine justice that would not allow a killer to escape, even from shipwreck.

verses.flow: But they were expecting him to be about to swell up or to fall down dead suddenly. And when they had waited for a long time and beheld nothing unusual happening to him, they changed their minds and said he was a god.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The swing from "murderer" to "a god" (θεόν, theon) mirrors the pattern at Lystra (Acts 14:11-13) where pagan peoples oscillated between hostility and deification. Paul's immunity to the viper fulfills the promise of the Lord in Luke 10:19 and Mark 16:18. The narrative demonstrates divine protection over the apostle as he moves toward Rome.

verses.flow: And it came to pass that the father of Publius lay sick, seized with fevers and dysentery; to whom Paul, having entered and having prayed, laid his hands upon him and healed him.
verses.note: GREEK: δυσεντερίῳ (dysenteriō, G1420) is a medical term for dysentery; Luke the physician uses precise terminology. The pattern of prayer followed by laying on of hands (ἐπιθεὶς τὰς χεῖρας) reflects apostolic healing practice throughout Acts (Acts 9:17; 19:11). TYPOLOGY: Paul's healing ministry on Malta echoes Elijah and Elisha among Gentiles (1 Kings 17; 2 Kings 5).

verses.flow: And after three months we set sail in a ship that had wintered at the island, an Alexandrian vessel, with the sign of the Dioscuri.
verses.note: GREEK: Διοσκούροις (Dioskourois, G1359) — "the Dioscuri," that is, Castor and Pollux, the twin sons of Zeus — were patron deities of sailors. παρασήμῳ (parasēmō, G3902) means "figurehead" or "sign" on the prow. HISTORICAL: Alexandrian grain ships were large vessels in Rome's vital grain supply chain from Egypt. This is the only NT occurrence of both Dioskourois and parasēmos.

verses.flow: And it came to pass that after three days he called together those who were the leading men of the Jews. And when they had assembled, he said to them, "Men, brothers, though I had done nothing against the people or the customs of our fathers, I was delivered as a prisoner from Jerusalem into the hands of the Romans,

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: who, having examined me, were desiring to release me, because there was no cause of death in me.

verses.flow: But when the Jews spoke against it, I was compelled to appeal to Caesar — not as having any accusation to bring against my nation.
For this reason therefore I urged you to see me and to speak with me, for on account of the hope of Israel I am bound with this chain."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The hope of Israel" (τῆς ἐλπίδος τοῦ Ἰσραήλ) is Paul's summary of his entire gospel message — the resurrection of the dead and the messianic fulfillment in Jesus (Acts 23:6; 24:15; 26:6-8). Paul's chains are directly linked to this hope, making him a prisoner not of Rome but of Israel's eschatological expectation fulfilled in Christ.

verses.flow: And having appointed him a day, they came to him at his lodging in greater numbers, to whom he was expounding, solemnly testifying the kingdom of God, and persuading them concerning Jesus, both from the Law of Moses and from the Prophets, from early morning until evening.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul's method — testifying the kingdom of God and persuading concerning Jesus from Moses and the Prophets — mirrors Jesus' own post-resurrection hermeneutic (Luke 24:27, 44). The phrase τὴν βασιλείαν τοῦ θεοῦ (the kingdom of God) bookends the entire Acts narrative (Acts 1:3; 28:23, 31). STRUCTURE: This verse forms the theological climax of Paul's Roman ministry, uniting Law, Prophets, kingdom, and Christ.

verses.flow: And being in disagreement with one another, they were departing, Paul having spoken one word: "Rightly did the Holy Spirit speak through Isaiah the prophet to your fathers,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul attributes Isaiah's words directly to the Holy Spirit (τὸ πνεῦμα τὸ ἅγιον), affirming divine authorship of Scripture. The same Isaiah 6:9-10 passage is quoted by Jesus (Matt 13:14-15; John 12:40) and cited by Paul here as the definitive explanation for Jewish unbelief — forming a consistent NT pattern of prophetic hardening theology.

verses.flow: saying, 'Go to this people and say: By hearing you shall hear and shall by no means understand, and seeing you shall see and shall by no means perceive;

for the heart of this people has grown dull, and with their ears they heard heavily, and their eyes they have closed, lest they should see with their eyes and hear with their ears and understand with their heart and turn back, and I shall heal them.'
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This quotation of Isaiah 6:9-10 (LXX) is the most extensively cited OT passage in the NT (Matt 13:14-15; Mark 4:12; Luke 8:10; John 12:40; Rom 11:8). ἐπαχύνθη (epachynthē) — "has grown fat/dull" — describes a judicial hardening. The promise "and I shall heal them" (ἰάσομαι αὐτούς) preserves the hope of eschatological restoration for Israel (Rom 11:25-26).

verses.flow: Let it be known to you therefore that this salvation of God has been sent to the nations; they also will hear.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse marks the pivotal turning point of salvation-history in Acts: the formal announcement that God's salvation (τὸ σωτήριον τοῦ θεοῦ) is sent to the nations (τοῖς ἔθνεσιν). The emphatic αὐτοὶ καὶ ἀκούσονται — "they also will hear" — contrasts Gentile receptivity with Jewish refusal, fulfilling the pattern announced in Acts 13:46 and 18:6.

verses.flow: And he remained a whole two years in his own rented dwelling, and was welcoming all those coming in to him,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: proclaiming the kingdom of God and teaching the things concerning the Lord Jesus Christ with all boldness, unhindered.
verses.note: GREEK: ἀκωλύτως (akōlytōs, G209) — "unhindered" — is a hapax legomenon in the NT, appearing only here. It is the final word of Acts and carries enormous rhetorical weight. THEOLOGICAL: The book ends with the kingdom of God being proclaimed and the Lord Jesus Christ being taught openly in the capital of the empire. STRUCTURE: Acts closes not with Paul's fate but with the unstoppable advance of the gospel — the kingdom preached ἀκωλύτως, unhindered, in Rome itself. The pairing of "the kingdom of God" and "the Lord Jesus Christ" forms the ultimate theological summary of the entire book.
